revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平}}, who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流}} of his elder son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Saneyori|ja|藤原実頼}}, and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流}} of his younger son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流}}, also emerged.
During the {{ill|insei|ja|院政}} period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実定}} and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房}}. In the late Heian period, {{ill|Matsudono Motofusa|ja|松殿基房}} synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|group="注釈"}} {{Reflist}}
== References ==
* {{Cite book|author=Ishimura Sadakichi|title=Yūsoku Kojitsu|editor=Arashi Yoshito|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1987|volume=2|isbn=978-4061588004}} * {{Cite book|author=Wada Hidematsu|title=Kanshoku Yōkai|editor=Tokoro Isao|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1983|isbn=978-4061586215}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Yūsoku|ja|有職}} * {{ill|Imperial rituals|ja|朝儀}} * {{ill|Miyauchi rites|ja|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Japanese governmental systems|ja|日本の官制}} * {{ill|Predecessor Traditions|ja|前賢故実}} * {{ill|Historical verification|ja|時代考証}}

{{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平}}, who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流}} of his elder son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Saneyori|ja|藤原実頼}}, and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流}} of his younger son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流}}, also emerged.
During the {{ill|Cloistered rule|ja|院政|Cloistered rule=Cloistered rule|lt=insei}} period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実定}} and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房}}. In the late Heian period, {{ill|Matsudono Motofusa|ja|松殿基房}} synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|group="注釈"}} {{Reflist}}
== References ==
* {{Cite book|author=Ishimura Sadakichi|title=Yūsoku Kojitsu|editor=Arashi Yoshito|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1987|volume=2|isbn=978-4061588004}} * {{Cite book|author=Wada Hidematsu|title=Kanshoku Yōkai|editor=Tokoro Isao|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1983|isbn=978-4061586215}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Yūsoku|ja|有職}} * {{ill|Imperial rituals|ja|朝儀}} * {{ill|Miyauchi rites|ja|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Japanese governmental systems|ja|日本の官制}} * {{ill|Predecessor Traditions|ja|前賢故実}} * {{ill|Historical verification|ja|時代考証}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] to [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平}}, who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流}} of his elder son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Saneyori|ja|藤原実頼}}, and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流}} of his younger son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流}}, also emerged.
During the {{ill|Cloistered rule|ja|院政|Cloistered rule=Cloistered rule|lt=insei}} period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実定}} and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房}}. In the late Heian period, {{ill|Matsudono Motofusa|ja|松殿基房}} synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|group="注釈"}} {{Reflist}}
== References ==
* {{Cite book|author=Ishimura Sadakichi|title=Yūsoku Kojitsu|editor=Arashi Yoshito|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1987|volume=2|isbn=978-4061588004}} * {{Cite book|author=Wada Hidematsu|title=Kanshoku Yōkai|editor=Tokoro Isao|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1983|isbn=978-4061586215}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Yūsoku|ja|有職}} * {{ill|Imperial rituals|ja|朝儀}} * {{ill|Miyauchi rites|ja|宮中祭祀}} * {{ill|Japanese governmental systems|ja|日本の官制}} * {{ill|Predecessor Traditions|ja|前賢故実}} * {{ill|Historical verification|ja|時代考証}}

{{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.

These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記}}. In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.

{{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平}}, who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流}} of his elder son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Saneyori|ja|藤原実頼}}, and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流}} of his younger son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流}}, also emerged.
During the {{ill|Cloistered rule|ja|院政|Cloistered rule=Cloistered rule|lt=insei}} period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実定}} and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房}}. In the late Heian period, {{ill|Matsudono Motofusa|ja|松殿基房}} synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.

{{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平}}, who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流}} of his elder son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Saneyori|ja|藤原実頼}}, and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流}} of his younger son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流}}, also emerged.
During the {{ill|Cloistered rule|ja|院政|Cloistered rule=Cloistered rule|lt=insei}} period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実定}} and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房}}. In the late Heian period, {{ill|Matsudono Motofusa|ja|松殿基房}} synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.
== Notes ==

revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平}}, who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流}} of his elder son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Saneyori|ja|藤原実頼}}, and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流}} of his younger son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流}}, also emerged.
During the {{ill|Cloistered rule|ja|院政|Cloistered rule=Cloistered rule|lt=insei}} period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実定}} and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房}}. In the late Heian period, {{ill|Matsudono Motofusa|ja|松殿基房}} synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.

{{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平}}, who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流}} of his elder son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Saneyori|ja|藤原実頼}}, and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流}} of his younger son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流}}, also emerged.
During the {{ill|Cloistered rule|ja|院政|Cloistered rule=Cloistered rule|lt=insei}} period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実定}} and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房}}. In the late Heian period, {{ill|Matsudono Motofusa|ja|松殿基房}} synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.

From the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平}}, who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流}} of his elder son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Saneyori|ja|藤原実頼}}, and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流}} of his younger son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流}}, also emerged.
During the {{ill|Cloistered rule|ja|院政|Cloistered rule=Cloistered rule|lt=insei}} period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実定}} and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房}}. In the late Heian period, {{ill|Matsudono Motofusa|ja|松殿基房}} synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.

'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平}}, who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流}} of his elder son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Saneyori|ja|藤原実頼}}, and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流}} of his younger son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流}}, also emerged.
During the {{ill|Cloistered rule|ja|院政|Cloistered rule=Cloistered rule|lt=insei}} period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実定}} and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房}}. In the late Heian period, {{ill|Matsudono Motofusa|ja|松殿基房}} synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.

'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平}}, who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流}} of his elder son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Saneyori|ja|藤原実頼}}, and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流}} of his younger son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流}}, also emerged.
During the {{ill|Cloistered rule|ja|院政|Cloistered rule=Cloistered rule|lt=insei}} period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実定}} and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房}}. In the late Heian period, {{ill|Matsudono Motofusa|ja|松殿基房}} synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.

{{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平}}, who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流}} of his elder son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Saneyori|ja|藤原実頼}}, and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流}} of his younger son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流}}, also emerged.
During the {{ill|Cloistered rule|ja|院政|Cloistered rule=Cloistered rule|lt=insei}} period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実定}} and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房}}. In the late Heian period, {{ill|Matsudono Motofusa|ja|松殿基房}} synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.

{{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平}}, who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流}} of his elder son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Saneyori|ja|藤原実頼}}, and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流}} of his younger son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流}}, also emerged.
During the {{ill|Cloistered rule|ja|院政|Cloistered rule=Cloistered rule|lt=insei}} period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実定}} and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房}}. In the late Heian period, {{ill|Matsudono Motofusa|ja|松殿基房}} synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.

{{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平}}, who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流}} of his elder son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Saneyori|ja|藤原実頼}}, and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流}} of his younger son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流}}, also emerged.
During the {{ill|Cloistered rule|ja|院政|Cloistered rule=Cloistered rule|lt=insei}} period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実定}} and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房}}. In the late Heian period, {{ill|Matsudono Motofusa|ja|松殿基房}} synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|group="注釈"}} {{Reflist}}
== References ==

As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.

{{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平}}, who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流}} of his elder son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Saneyori|ja|藤原実頼}}, and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流}} of his younger son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔}}.<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流}}, also emerged.
During the {{ill|Cloistered rule|ja|院政|Cloistered rule=Cloistered rule|lt=insei}} period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実定}} and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房}}. In the late Heian period, {{ill|Matsudono Motofusa|ja|松殿基房}} synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松}}.

'''有職故実'''（ゆうそくこじつ）とは、古来の先例に基づいた、[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]や[[公家]]、[[武家]]の[[イベント|行事]]や[[法令]]・[[制度]]・[[風俗]]・[[習慣]]・[[官職]]・[[儀式]]・[[装束]]などのこと。また、それらを[[研究]]すること。
== 概説 == 「有識」とは過去の先例に関する[[知識]]を指し、「故実」とは公私の行動の是非に関する説得力のある根拠・規範の類を指す。そうした知識に通じた者を'''有識者'''（ゆうそくしゃ）と呼んだ。後に転じて「有職」と呼ぶようになった。
== 江戸時代前 == === 公家故実 === [[平安時代]]の中期から先例を伝える知識の体系化が進み、[[藤原忠平]]の執政期に儀礼の基本形が確立した。忠平は[[本康親王]]・[[貞保親王]]を通じて受け継いだ父[[藤原基経]]の知識、兄[[藤原時平]]の説、勅命や[[外記日記]]を参照して合理的な儀礼体系を作り上げた。忠平の知識は口伝によって二人の子に受け継がれ、兄[[藤原実頼|実頼]]の[[小野宮流]]、弟[[藤原師輔|師輔]]の[[九条流]]という2大儀礼流派が生まれた<ref>[[大津透]]『道長と宮廷社会 日本の歴史06』講談社学術文庫 22p ISBN 978-4062689069</ref>。また忠平五男[[藤原師尹|師尹]]の[[小一条流]]も発生し、のちに九条流から[[御堂流]]が成立した。[[院政期]]には[[源師頼]]を祖とする[[土御門流]]（[[村上源氏]]系）と[[源有仁]]を祖として縁戚の[[徳大寺実定]]・[[三条実房]]が完成させた[[花園流]]（[[閑院流]]系）もあったとされている。平安時代末期の関白[[松殿基房]]は、これら多数ある公家有職故実を集約し、のちに「松殿関白説」と呼ばれる学説にまとめられた。松殿関白説は長く公家社会で重要視され、二百年以上経った室町時代に[[後花園天皇]]の笏を新調するにあたり、[[二条持基]]が参考にした記録が残っている。
後に[[官司請負制]]が浸透すると、有職故実を[[家職]]とする家（[[徳大寺家]]（九条流）、[[大炊御門家]]（御堂流））も現れた。
有職故実に関する原点は官製の[[儀式書]]に由来を求めることが出来るが、後に貴族は自己の[[日記]]に有職故実を書き残し、子孫が代々の日記を集成して有職故実書を編纂するようになった。有職故実書の中でも、[[源高明]]の『[[西宮記]]』、[[藤原公任]]の『[[北山抄]]』、[[大江匡房]]の『[[江家次第]]』の三書は「後世の亀鑑」と仰がれ別格扱いであった。[[室町時代]]の[[一条兼良]]はこの三書を、『西宮記』は古礼、『北山抄』は[[一条天皇]]の時代以降の儀式、『江家次第』は[[後三条天皇]]の時代以後の儀式と記している。
鎌倉時代以降には専門的な研究が盛んとなり、儀式については[[順徳天皇]]の『[[禁秘抄]]』、[[後醍醐天皇]]の『[[建武年中行事]]』、[[一条兼良]]の『[[公事根源]]』、官職制度については[[北畠親房]]の『[[職原抄]]』、服飾については源雅亮の『雅亮装束抄』などの有職故実書が著された。また、著名な公家研究家としては[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]の[[洞院公賢]]・[[二条良基]]、[[室町時代]]の[[甘露寺親長]]、[[安土桃山時代|安土桃山]]・[[江戸時代]]初期の[[平田職忠]]、また江戸時代の野宮定基・平松時方などが挙げられる。
=== 武家故実 === [[平安時代]]には、武人の故実（武官故実）は、[[紀氏]]と[[大伴氏|伴氏]]が伝えていたが、[[武士]]の台頭とともに衰えた。
[[鎌倉時代]]には、[[源頼朝]]が故実に通じた武士を重んじ、故実の復元を図っている。以降、京都から断片的に流入した武官故実と関東在来の武士の慣習が合わさって、武家故実が体系化されていった。武家故実の中でも弓馬や軍陣における実践的な故実と幕府や主君の前における儀礼や作法などの故実が存在したが、戦法の変化によって前者は形式的なものになったのに対して、後者は公家故実とも融合して、[[室町時代]]に[[小笠原流]]や[[伊勢流]]が生まれた。
== 江戸時代 == [[江戸時代]]以後、古典研究の発展に伴って民間でも有職故実の研究をする者が現れ、世襲化されて学問としては停滞が見られた公家や武家の有職故実の伝統に囚われない独自の研究が見られた。[[河内国]]の[[壺井義知]]は民間の出ながら、官職・装束の研究においては当代随一とされて、公家さえもが教えを乞うたと言われている。公家達の側でも壺井に触発されるかのように、[[平松時方]]・[[野宮定基]]・[[滋野井公麗]]・[[裏松光世]]（[[裏松固禅|固禅]]）らによってその学問的水準が高められるようになった。武家側でも[[田安宗武]]・[[松平定信]]・[[塙保己一]]などが研究家として知られている。
武家故実についても、江戸時代の武家故実の第一人者とされる[[伊勢貞丈]]（[[伊勢氏]]一族）が多数の著作を著わしたほか、『[[本朝軍器考]]』を著わした[[新井白石]]・[[榊原長俊]]（伊勢貞丈の弟子）・[[中山信名]]（塙保己一の弟子）などが研究家として知られている。
== 近代以後 == 近代に入ると、旧来の公家・武家制度の廃止によって実用的な意味での有識故実研究の歴史は幕を閉じた。また、官制などの研究は[[法制史]]の一部となった。それでも[[歴史学]]の一環、あるいは日本文学研究の一環として研究が行われ続け、[[佐藤誠実]]・[[和田英松]]などの優れた研究家を輩出した。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''注釈''' {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} '''出典''' <!-- 文献参照ページ --> {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == <!-- 実際に参考にした文献一覧 --> *石村貞吉『有職故実』 嵐義人校訂（[[講談社学術文庫]]（上下）、1987年8月） ISBN 978-4061588004、ISBN 978-4061588011 *[[和田英松]]『官職要解』 [[所功]]校訂（講談社学術文庫、1983年11月） ISBN 978-4-06-158621-5
== 関連項目 == * [[有職]] * [[朝儀]] * [[宮中祭祀]] * [[日本の官制]] * [[前賢故実]] * [[時代考証]]
{{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ゆうそくこしつ}} [[Category:日本の文化]] [[Category:日本の制度史]] [[Category:儀式]] [[Category:有職故実書|*]] [[Category:主知主義]] [[Category:日本の史学史]] [[Category:日本の歴史資料]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-{{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of {{ill|Fujiwara no Tadahira|ja|藤原忠平|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|ja|藤原基経|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and his elder brother, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|ja|藤原時平|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of his elder son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Saneyori|ja|藤原実頼|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of his younger son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Morosuke|ja|藤原師輔|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, also emerged.
During the {{ill|Cloistered rule|ja|院政|Cloistered rule=Cloistered rule|lt=insei|qq=draft|qq=draft}} period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as {{ill|Tokudaiji Sanetsune|ja|徳大寺実定|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In the late Heian period, {{ill|Matsudono Motofusa|ja|松殿基房|qq=draft|qq=draft}} synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|qq=draft|qq=draft}} became widespread, some families such as the {{ill|Tokudaiji family|ja|徳大寺家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the {{ill|Ogasawara school|ja|小笠原流|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流|qq=draft|qq=draft}} emerged during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Edo Period ==
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|qq=draft|qq=draft}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|公家|1=Kuge|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|qq=draft|qq=draft}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|qq=draft|qq=draft}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|group="Note"}} {{Reflist}}
== References ==
* {{Cite book|author=Ishimura Sadakichi|title=Yūsoku Kojitsu|editor=Arashi Yoshito|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1987|volume=2|isbn=978-4061588004}} * {{Cite book|author=Wada Hidematsu|title=Kanshoku Yōkai|editor=Tokoro Isao|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1983|isbn=978-4061586215}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yūsoku|ja|有職|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Imperial rituals|ja|朝儀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Miyauchi rites|ja|宮中祭祀|1=Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|lt=Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Japanese governmental systems|ja|日本の官制|1=Japanese administrative system|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Zenken Kojitsu|ja|前賢故実|fr|Zenken kojitsu|ja|前賢故実|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Historical verification|ja|時代考証|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court|qq=}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|qq=}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tadahira|Fujiwara no Tadahira]], who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, [[:en:Fujiwara no Mototsune|Fujiwara no Mototsune]], and his elder brother, [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki diaries|qq=}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Miya school|qq=}} of his elder son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Saneyori|Fujiwara no Saneyori]], and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujo school|qq=}} of his younger son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsu Toru|qq=}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koitijo school|qq=}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mido school|qq=}}, also emerged.
During the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado school|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono school|qq=}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as [[:en:Tokudaiji Sanesada|Tokudaiji Sanetsune]] and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Sanefusa|qq=}}. In the late Heian period, [[:en:Matsudono Motofusa|Matsudono Motofusa]] synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanji-uke|qq=}} became widespread, some families such as the [[:en:Tokudaiji family|Tokudaiji family]] (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi no Mikado family|qq=}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the [[:en:Ogasawara-ryū|Ogasawara school]] and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise school|qq=}} emerged during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Edo Period ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Yoshitomo|qq=}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|qq=}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|qq=}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|qq=}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|qq=}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical studies|qq=}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato Makoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Hidematsu|qq=}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|group="Note"}} {{Reflist}}
== References ==
* {{Cite book|author=Ishimura Sadakichi|title=Yūsoku Kojitsu|editor=Arashi Yoshito|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1987|volume=2|isbn=978-4061588004}} * {{Cite book|author=Wada Hidematsu|title=Kanshoku Yōkai|editor=Tokoro Isao|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1983|isbn=978-4061586215}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yūsoku|ja|有職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku|qq=}} * {{ill|Imperial rituals|ja|朝儀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial rituals|qq=}} * [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] * {{ill|Japanese governmental systems|ja|日本の官制|1=Japanese administrative system|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese governmental systems|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese administrative system|qq=}} * [[:en:Zenken Kojitsu|Zenken Kojitsu]] * {{ill|Historical verification|ja|時代考証|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical verification|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|有職故実|version=102894465|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Yūsoku Kojitsu]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:History of Japanese institutions]] [[Category:Ceremonies]] [[Category:Yūsoku Kojitsu texts]] [[Category:Rationalism]] [[Category:History of Japanese historiography]] [[Category:Historical records of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese-history-stub]] [[Category:qq]]<!--Doing this to bump these pages because my history retrieval attempts have led to category caching errors-->[[sn:Yūsoku Kojitsu]][[Category:qq]] }}

From the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tadahira|Fujiwara no Tadahira]], who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, [[:en:Fujiwara no Mototsune|Fujiwara no Mototsune]], and his elder brother, [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki diaries|qq=}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Miya school|qq=}} of his elder son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Saneyori|Fujiwara no Saneyori]], and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujo school|qq=}} of his younger son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsu Toru|qq=}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koitijo school|qq=}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mido school|qq=}}, also emerged.
During the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado school|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono school|qq=}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as [[:en:Tokudaiji Sanesada|Tokudaiji Sanetsune]] and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Sanefusa|qq=}}. In the late Heian period, [[:en:Matsudono Motofusa|Matsudono Motofusa]] synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanji-uke|qq=}} became widespread, some families such as the [[:en:Tokudaiji family|Tokudaiji family]] (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi no Mikado family|qq=}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the [[:en:Ogasawara-ryū|Ogasawara school]] and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise school|qq=}} emerged during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Edo Period ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Yoshitomo|qq=}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|qq=}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|qq=}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|qq=}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|qq=}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical studies|qq=}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato Makoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Hidematsu|qq=}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|group="Note"}} {{Reflist}}
== References ==

{{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court|qq=}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|qq=}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tadahira|Fujiwara no Tadahira]], who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, [[:en:Fujiwara no Mototsune|Fujiwara no Mototsune]], and his elder brother, [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki diaries|qq=}}. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, [[:en:Fujiwara no Mototsune|Fujiwara no Mototsune]], and his elder brother, [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki diaries|qq=}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Miya school|qq=}} of his elder son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Saneyori|Fujiwara no Saneyori]], and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujo school|qq=}} of his younger son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsu Toru|qq=}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koitijo school|qq=}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mido school|qq=}}, also emerged.
During the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado school|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono school|qq=}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as [[:en:Tokudaiji Sanesada|Tokudaiji Sanetsune]] and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Sanefusa|qq=}}. In the late Heian period, [[:en:Matsudono Motofusa|Matsudono Motofusa]] synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanji-uke|qq=}} became widespread, some families such as the [[:en:Tokudaiji family|Tokudaiji family]] (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi no Mikado family|qq=}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the [[:en:Ogasawara-ryū|Ogasawara school]] and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise school|qq=}} emerged during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Edo Period ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Yoshitomo|qq=}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|qq=}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|qq=}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|qq=}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|qq=}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical studies|qq=}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato Makoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Hidematsu|qq=}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|group="Note"}} {{Reflist}}
== References ==
