{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q1623327]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tadahira|Fujiwara no Tadahira]], who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, [[:en:Fujiwara no Mototsune|Fujiwara no Mototsune]], and his elder brother, [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki diaries|qq=}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Miya school|qq=|1=Onomiya-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463986}} of his elder son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Saneyori|Fujiwara no Saneyori]], and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujo school|qq=|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} of his younger son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsu Toru|qq=|1=Tōru Ōtsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437382}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koitijo school|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mido school|qq=|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}}, also emerged.
During the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado school|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono school|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as [[:en:Tokudaiji Sanesada|Tokudaiji Sanetsune]] and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Sanefusa|qq=|1=Sanjō Sanefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355831}}. In the late Heian period, [[:en:Matsudono Motofusa|Matsudono Motofusa]] synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanji-uke|qq=|1=Kanshi Chōdai Seido|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452090}} became widespread, some families such as the [[:en:Tokudaiji family|Tokudaiji family]] (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi no Mikado family|qq=|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the [[:en:Ogasawara-ryū|Ogasawara school]] and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise school|qq=|1=Ise-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700593}} emerged during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Edo Period ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Yoshitomo|qq=|1=Tsuboi Yoshichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6536790}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|qq=|1=Sadatake Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379125}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|qq=|1=Kozen Uramatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626763}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical studies|qq=|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato Makoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Hidematsu|qq=|1=Hidematsu Wada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418117}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|group="Note"}} {{Reflist}}
== References ==
* {{Cite book|author=Ishimura Sadakichi|title=Yūsoku Kojitsu|editor=Arashi Yoshito|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1987|volume=2|isbn=978-4061588004}} * {{Cite book|author=Wada Hidematsu|title=Kanshoku Yōkai|editor=Tokoro Isao|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1983|isbn=978-4061586215}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yūsoku|ja|有職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku|qq=|1=Yūsoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11516660}} * {{ill|Imperial rituals|ja|朝儀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial rituals|qq=}} * [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] * {{ill|Japanese governmental systems|ja|日本の官制|1=structure of the ritsuryō government|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese governmental systems|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese administrative system|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193883}} * [[:en:Zenken Kojitsu|Zenken Kojitsu]] * {{ill|Historical verification|ja|時代考証|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical verification|qq=|1=Historical verification|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11514337}}

These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, [[:en:Fujiwara no Mototsune|Fujiwara no Mototsune]], and his elder brother, [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki diaries|qq=}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Miya school|qq=|1=Onomiya-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463986}} of his elder son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Saneyori|Fujiwara no Saneyori]], and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujo school|qq=|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} of his younger son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsu Toru|qq=|1=Tōru Ōtsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437382}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koitijo school|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mido school|qq=|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}}, also emerged.
During the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado school|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono school|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as [[:en:Tokudaiji Sanesada|Tokudaiji Sanetsune]] and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Sanefusa|qq=|1=Sanjō Sanefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355831}}. In the late Heian period, [[:en:Matsudono Motofusa|Matsudono Motofusa]] synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanji-uke|qq=|1=Kanshi Chōdai Seido|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452090}} became widespread, some families such as the [[:en:Tokudaiji family|Tokudaiji family]] (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi no Mikado family|qq=|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the [[:en:Ogasawara-ryū|Ogasawara school]] and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise school|qq=|1=Ise-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700593}} emerged during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Edo Period ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Yoshitomo|qq=|1=Tsuboi Yoshichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6536790}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|qq=|1=Sadatake Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379125}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|qq=|1=Kozen Uramatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626763}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical studies|qq=|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato Makoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Hidematsu|qq=|1=Hidematsu Wada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418117}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Hofzeremoniell]][[ja:有職故実]][[ko:유직고실]][[zh:有职故实]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q1623327]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tadahira|Fujiwara no Tadahira]], who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, [[:en:Fujiwara no Mototsune|Fujiwara no Mototsune]], and his elder brother, [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki diaries|qq=}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Miya school|qq=|1=Onomiya-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463986}} of his elder son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Saneyori|Fujiwara no Saneyori]], and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujo school|qq=|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} of his younger son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsu Toru|qq=|1=Tōru Ōtsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437382}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koitijo school|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mido school|qq=|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}}, also emerged.
During the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado school|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono school|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as [[:en:Tokudaiji Sanesada|Tokudaiji Sanetsune]] and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Sanefusa|qq=|1=Sanjō Sanefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355831}}. In the late Heian period, [[:en:Matsudono Motofusa|Matsudono Motofusa]] synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanji-uke|qq=|1=Kanshi Chōdai Seido|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452090}} became widespread, some families such as the [[:en:Tokudaiji family|Tokudaiji family]] (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi no Mikado family|qq=|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the [[:en:Ogasawara-ryū|Ogasawara school]] and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise school|qq=|1=Ise-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700593}} emerged during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Edo Period ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Yoshitomo|qq=|1=Tsuboi Yoshichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6536790}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. [[ja:有職故実]] [[Category:Intellectualism]] [[Category:Rituals]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese institutions]] [[Category:Historiography of Japan]] [[Category:Culture of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese Historical Materials]] [[Category:Yusoku Kojitsu Texts]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[de:Hofzeremoniell]] [[ko:유직고실]] [[zh:有职故实]] {{wikidata link|Q1623327}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Hofzeremoniell]][[ja:有職故実]][[ko:유직고실]][[zh:有职故实]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q1623327]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tadahira|Fujiwara no Tadahira]], who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, [[:en:Fujiwara no Mototsune|Fujiwara no Mototsune]], and his elder brother, [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki diaries|qq=}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Miya school|1=Onomiya-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463986}} of his elder son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Saneyori|Fujiwara no Saneyori]], and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujo school|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} of his younger son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsu Toru|1=Tōru Ōtsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437382}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koitijo school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mido school|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}}, also emerged.
During the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado school|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as [[:en:Tokudaiji Sanesada|Tokudaiji Sanetsune]] and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Sanefusa|1=Sanjō Sanefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355831}}. In the late Heian period, [[:en:Matsudono Motofusa|Matsudono Motofusa]] synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanji-uke|1=Kanshi Chōdai Seido|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452090}} became widespread, some families such as the [[:en:Tokudaiji family|Tokudaiji family]] (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi no Mikado family|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the [[:en:Ogasawara-ryū|Ogasawara school]] and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise school|1=Ise-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700593}} emerged during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Edo Period ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Yoshitomo|1=Tsuboi Yoshichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6536790}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|1=Sadatake Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379125}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|1=Kozen Uramatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626763}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical studies|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato Makoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Hidematsu|1=Hidematsu Wada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418117}}.
== Notes ==

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Yoshitomo|1=Tsuboi Yoshichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6536790}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|1=Sadatake Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379125}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|1=Kozen Uramatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626763}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical studies|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato Makoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Hidematsu|1=Hidematsu Wada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418117}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|group="Note"}} {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Hofzeremoniell]][[ja:有職故実]][[ko:유직고실]][[zh:有职故实]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q1623327]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tadahira|Fujiwara no Tadahira]], who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, [[:en:Fujiwara no Mototsune|Fujiwara no Mototsune]], and his elder brother, [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki diaries|qq=}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Miya school|1=Onomiya-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463986}} of his elder son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Saneyori|Fujiwara no Saneyori]], and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujo school|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} of his younger son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsu Toru|1=Tōru Ōtsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437382}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koitijo school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mido school|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}}, also emerged.
During the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado school|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as [[:en:Tokudaiji Sanesada|Tokudaiji Sanetsune]] and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Sanefusa|1=Sanjō Sanefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355831}}. In the late Heian period, [[:en:Matsudono Motofusa|Matsudono Motofusa]] synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanji-uke|1=Kanshi Chōdai Seido|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452090}} became widespread, some families such as the [[:en:Tokudaiji family|Tokudaiji family]] (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi no Mikado family|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the [[:en:Ogasawara-ryū|Ogasawara school]] and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise school|1=Ise-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700593}} emerged during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Edo Period ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Yoshitomo|1=Tsuboi Yoshichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6536790}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|1=Sadatake Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379125}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|1=Kozen Uramatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626763}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical studies|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato Makoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Hidematsu|1=Hidematsu Wada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418117}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|group="Note"}} {{Reflist}}
== References ==

{{translated page|ja|有職故実|version=102894465|comment=Imported full ja history}}

This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Miya school|1=Onomiya-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463986}} of his elder son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Saneyori|Fujiwara no Saneyori]], and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujo school|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} of his younger son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsu Toru|1=Tōru Ōtsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437382}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koitijo school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mido school|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}}, also emerged.
During the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado school|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as [[:en:Tokudaiji Sanesada|Tokudaiji Sanetsune]] and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Sanefusa|1=Sanjō Sanefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355831}}. In the late Heian period, [[:en:Matsudono Motofusa|Matsudono Motofusa]] synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanji-uke|1=Kanshi Chōdai Seido|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452090}} became widespread, some families such as the [[:en:Tokudaiji family|Tokudaiji family]] (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi no Mikado family|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the [[:en:Ogasawara-ryū|Ogasawara school]] and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise school|1=Ise-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700593}} emerged during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Edo Period ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Yoshitomo|1=Tsuboi Yoshichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6536790}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|1=Sadatake Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379125}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|1=Kozen Uramatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626763}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical studies|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato Makoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Hidematsu|1=Hidematsu Wada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418117}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Hofzeremoniell]][[ja:有職故実]][[ko:유직고실]][[zh:有职故实]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q1623327]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tadahira|Fujiwara no Tadahira]], who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, [[:en:Fujiwara no Mototsune|Fujiwara no Mototsune]], and his elder brother, [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki diaries|qq=}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Miya school|1=Onomiya-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463986}} of his elder son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Saneyori|Fujiwara no Saneyori]], and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujo school|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} of his younger son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsu Toru|1=Tōru Ōtsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437382}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koitijo school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mido school|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}}, also emerged.
During the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado school|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as [[:en:Tokudaiji Sanesada|Tokudaiji Sanetsune]] and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Sanefusa|1=Sanjō Sanefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355831}}. In the late Heian period, [[:en:Matsudono Motofusa|Matsudono Motofusa]] synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanji-uke|1=Kanshi Chōdai Seido|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452090}} became widespread, some families such as the [[:en:Tokudaiji family|Tokudaiji family]] (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi no Mikado family|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.

In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|1=Sadatake Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379125}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|1=Kozen Uramatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626763}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Hofzeremoniell]][[ja:有職故実]][[ko:유직고실]][[zh:有职故实]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q1623327]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tadahira|Fujiwara no Tadahira]], who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, [[:en:Fujiwara no Mototsune|Fujiwara no Mototsune]], and his elder brother, [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki diaries|qq=}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Miya school|1=Onomiya-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463986}} of his elder son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Saneyori|Fujiwara no Saneyori]], and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujo school|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} of his younger son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsu Toru|1=Tōru Ōtsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437382}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koitijo school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mido school|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}}, also emerged.
During the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado school|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as [[:en:Tokudaiji Sanesada|Tokudaiji Sanetsune]] and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Sanefusa|1=Sanjō Sanefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355831}}. In the late Heian period, [[:en:Matsudono Motofusa|Matsudono Motofusa]] synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanji-uke|1=Kanshi Chōdai Seido|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452090}} became widespread, some families such as the [[:en:Tokudaiji family|Tokudaiji family]] (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi no Mikado family|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the [[:en:Ogasawara-ryū|Ogasawara school]] and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise school|1=Ise-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700593}} emerged during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Edo Period ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Yoshitomo|1=Tsuboi Yoshichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6536790}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|1=Sadatake Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379125}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|1=Kozen Uramatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626763}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical studies|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato Makoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Hidematsu|1=Hidematsu Wada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418117}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|group="Note"}} {{Reflist}}
== References ==
* {{Cite book|author=Ishimura Sadakichi|title=Yūsoku Kojitsu|editor=Arashi Yoshito|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1987|volume=2|isbn=978-4061588004}} * {{Cite book|author=Wada Hidematsu|title=East Asian government position Yōkai|editor=Tokoro Isao|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1983|isbn=978-4061586215}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yūsoku|ja|有職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku|1=Yūsoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11516660}} * {{ill|Imperial rituals|ja|朝儀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial rituals|qq=}} * [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] * {{ill|Japanese governmental systems|ja|日本の官制|1=structure of the ritsuryō government|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese governmental systems|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese administrative system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193883}} * [[:en:Zenken Kojitsu|Zenken Kojitsu]] * {{ill|Historical verification|ja|時代考証|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical verification|1=Historical verification|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11514337}}

However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical studies|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato Makoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Hidematsu|1=Hidematsu Wada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418117}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|group="Note"}} {{Reflist}}
== References ==
* {{Cite book|author=Ishimura Sadakichi|title=Yūsoku Kojitsu|editor=Arashi Yoshito|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1987|volume=2|isbn=978-4061588004}} * {{Cite book|author=Wada Hidematsu|title=East Asian government position Yōkai|editor=Tokoro Isao|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1983|isbn=978-4061586215}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yūsoku|ja|有職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku|1=Yūsoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11516660}} * {{ill|Imperial rituals|ja|朝儀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial rituals|qq=}} * [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] * {{ill|Japanese governmental systems|ja|日本の官制|1=structure of the ritsuryō government|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese governmental systems|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese administrative system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193883}} * [[:en:Zenken Kojitsu|Zenken Kojitsu]] * {{ill|Historical verification|ja|時代考証|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical verification|1=Historical verification|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11514337}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Hofzeremoniell]][[ja:有職故実]][[ko:유직고실]][[zh:有职故实]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q1623327]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tadahira|Fujiwara no Tadahira]], who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, [[:en:Fujiwara no Mototsune|Fujiwara no Mototsune]], and his elder brother, [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki diaries|qq=}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Miya school|1=Onomiya-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463986}} of his elder son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Saneyori|Fujiwara no Saneyori]], and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujo school|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} of his younger son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsu Toru|1=Tōru Ōtsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437382}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koitijo school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mido school|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}}, also emerged.
During the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado school|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as [[:en:Tokudaiji Sanesada|Tokudaiji Sanetsune]] and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Sanefusa|1=Sanjō Sanefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355831}}. In the late Heian period, [[:en:Matsudono Motofusa|Matsudono Motofusa]] synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanji-uke|1=Kanshi Chōdai Seido|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452090}} became widespread, some families such as the [[:en:Tokudaiji family|Tokudaiji family]] (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi no Mikado family|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the [[:en:Ogasawara-ryū|Ogasawara school]] and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise school|1=Ise-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700593}} emerged during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Edo Period ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Yoshitomo|1=Tsuboi Yoshichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6536790}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|1=Sadatake Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379125}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|1=Kozen Uramatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626763}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical studies|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato Makoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Hidematsu|1=Hidematsu Wada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418117}}.
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|group="Note"}} {{Reflist}}
== References ==
* {{Cite book|author=Ishimura Sadakichi|title=Yūsoku Kojitsu|editor=Arashi Yoshito|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1987|volume=2|isbn=978-4061588004}} * {{Cite book|author=Wada Hidematsu|title=East Asian government position Yōkai|editor=Tokoro Isao|series=Kodansha Academic Library|year=1983|isbn=978-4061586215}} == See Also == * {{ill|Yūsoku|ja|有職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yūsoku|1=Yūsoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11516660}} * {{ill|Imperial rituals|ja|朝儀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial rituals|qq=}} * [[:en:Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Ceremonies of the Japanese Imperial Palace]] * {{ill|Japanese governmental systems|ja|日本の官制|1=structure of the ritsuryō government|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese governmental systems|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese administrative system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193883}} * [[:en:Zenken Kojitsu|Zenken Kojitsu]] * {{ill|Historical verification|ja|時代考証|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical verification|1=Historical verification|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11514337}}
{{translated page|ja|有職故実|version=102894465|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Schools like the [[:en:Ogasawara-ryū|Ogasawara school]] and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise school|1=Ise-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700593}} emerged during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Edo Period ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Yoshitomo|1=Tsuboi Yoshichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6536790}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|1=Sadatake Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379125}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|1=Kozen Uramatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626763}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical studies|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato Makoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Hidematsu|1=Hidematsu Wada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418117}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Hofzeremoniell]][[ja:有職故実]][[ko:유직고실]][[zh:有职故实]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Hofzeremoniell]][[ja:有職故実]][[ko:유직고실]][[zh:有职故实]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Hofzeremoniell]][[ja:有職故実]][[ko:유직고실]][[zh:有职故实]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q1623327]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=July 2023}}
'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tadahira|Fujiwara no Tadahira]], who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, [[:en:Fujiwara no Mototsune|Fujiwara no Mototsune]], and his elder brother, [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki diaries|qq=}}. This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Miya school|1=Onomiya-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463986}} of his elder son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Saneyori|Fujiwara no Saneyori]], and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujo school|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} of his younger son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsu Toru|1=Tōru Ōtsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437382}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koitijo school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mido school|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}}, also emerged.
During the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado school|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as [[:en:Tokudaiji Sanesada|Tokudaiji Sanetsune]] and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Sanefusa|1=Sanjō Sanefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355831}}. In the late Heian period, [[:en:Matsudono Motofusa|Matsudono Motofusa]] synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanji-uke|1=Kanshi Chōdai Seido|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452090}} became widespread, some families such as the [[:en:Tokudaiji family|Tokudaiji family]] (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi no Mikado family|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the [[:en:Ogasawara-ryū|Ogasawara school]] and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise school|1=Ise-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700593}} emerged during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Edo Period ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Yoshitomo|1=Tsuboi Yoshichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6536790}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|1=Sadatake Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379125}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|1=Kozen Uramatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626763}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical studies|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato Makoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Hidematsu|1=Hidematsu Wada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418117}}.
== Notes ==

This tradition was orally transmitted to his sons, forming two major schools of court ritual: the {{ill|Ono no Miya school|ja|小野宮流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Miya school|1=Onomiya-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463986}} of his elder son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Saneyori|Fujiwara no Saneyori]], and the {{ill|Kujo school|ja|九条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujo school|1=Kujo school|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370406}} of his younger son, [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]].<ref>{{ill|Otsu Toru|ja|大津透|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otsu Toru|1=Tōru Ōtsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437382}}, ''Michinaga and the Court Society: Japanese History 06'', Kodansha Academic Library, ISBN 978-4062689069.</ref> Other schools, such as the {{ill|Koitijo school|ja|小一条流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koitijo school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Morosuke’s fifth son and the later {{ill|Mido school|ja|御堂流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mido school|1=Midō-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488901}}, also emerged.
During the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado school|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as [[:en:Tokudaiji Sanesada|Tokudaiji Sanetsune]] and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Sanefusa|1=Sanjō Sanefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355831}}. In the late Heian period, [[:en:Matsudono Motofusa|Matsudono Motofusa]] synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanji-uke|1=Kanshi Chōdai Seido|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452090}} became widespread, some families such as the [[:en:Tokudaiji family|Tokudaiji family]] (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi no Mikado family|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.
=== Bushi Traditions ===
In the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] emphasized bushi traditions. Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. Schools like the [[:en:Ogasawara-ryū|Ogasawara school]] and {{ill|Ise school|ja|伊勢流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise school|1=Ise-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18700593}} emerged during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].
== Edo Period ==
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], independent researchers such as {{ill|Tsuboi Yoshitomo|ja|壺井義知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Yoshitomo|1=Tsuboi Yoshichika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6536790}} gained recognition. Influential figures from the [[:en:Kuge|Kuge]] and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}} further elevated the academic level of these studies. Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|1=Sadatake Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379125}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|1=Kozen Uramatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626763}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
== Modern Period ==
With the abolition of traditional court and samurai systems in the modern period, yūsoku kojitsu lost its practical significance. However, it continued to be studied as part of {{ill|historical studies|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/historical studies|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} or Japanese literary studies, with contributions from scholars like {{ill|Sato Makoto|ja|佐藤誠実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sato Makoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Wada Hidematsu|ja|和田英松|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Hidematsu|1=Hidematsu Wada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418117}}.

'''Yūsoku Kojitsu''' (有職故実) refers to the study and traditions of ancient customs, regulations, systems, etiquette, ceremonies, attire, and practices of the {{ill|Imperial Court|ja|朝廷 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Court|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Imperial Court of Japan|WD=Q30253177}} and {{ill|samurai class|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai class|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buke|WD=Q2927809}}. The term also encompasses the study of these traditions.
== Overview ==
"Yūsoku" denotes knowledge of past precedents, while "kojitsu" refers to persuasive grounds or norms regarding public and private conduct. Individuals well-versed in these areas were referred to as '''yūsokusha''' (有職者), a term later shortened to "yūsoku."
== Pre-Edo Period ==
=== Court Traditions ===
From the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the systematization of precedents advanced under the regency of [[:en:Fujiwara no Tadahira|Fujiwara no Tadahira]], who established the basic forms of court rituals. These rituals were rationalized using the knowledge of Tadahira’s father, [[:en:Fujiwara no Mototsune|Fujiwara no Mototsune]], and his elder brother, [[:en:Fujiwara no Tokihira|Fujiwara no Tokihira]], along with imperial orders and references to {{ill|Geki diaries|ja|外記日記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geki diaries|qq=}}. During the [[:en:Cloistered rule|insei]] period, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado school|ja|土御門流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado school|qq=}} and the {{ill|Hanazono school|ja|花園流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono school|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were formed, the latter completed by figures such as [[:en:Tokudaiji Sanesada|Tokudaiji Sanetsune]] and {{ill|Sanjō Sanefusa|ja|三条実房|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Sanefusa|1=Sanjō Sanefusa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11355831}}. In the late Heian period, [[:en:Matsudono Motofusa|Matsudono Motofusa]] synthesized many traditions into what became known as the "Matsudono Kanpaku Theory," which influenced court society for centuries.
As {{ill|kanji-uke|ja|官司請負制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanji-uke|1=Kanshi Chōdai Seido|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452090}} became widespread, some families such as the [[:en:Tokudaiji family|Tokudaiji family]] (Kujo school) and the {{ill|Ōi no Mikado family|ja|大炊御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi no Mikado family|1=Ōinomikado family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11437712}} (Mido school) adopted yūsoku kojitsu as their professional legacy.

Over time, practices from Kyoto and local customs blended to form the foundations of samurai etiquette. == Edo Period ==

Samurai experts like {{ill|Ise Sadatake|ja|伊勢貞丈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Sadatake|1=Sadatake Ise|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379125}} contributed to many texts, while court nobles such as {{ill|Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja|平松時方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiramatsu Tokikata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Uramatsu Kōyo|ja|裏松光世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramatsu Kōyo|1=Kozen Uramatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626763}} advanced public and private yūsoku traditions.
